content:
The $0 Stack I Used to Build 3 Apps in 30 Days as a Solo Developer
People always ask me what tools I use.
They expect some expensive SaaS subscriptions, a fancy cloud setup, maybe a team behind me. When I tell them my entire infrastructure costs me essentially nothing to start, they don’t believe it at first.
But here’s the truth: the barrier to shipping apps in 2025 is not money. It never really was. The barrier is decisions. Too many tools, too many options, too many YouTube tutorials telling you different things.
I spent weeks paralyzed by that. Trying to pick the "perfect" stack before writing a single line of code.
Then I stopped overthinking and just picked one stack. Used it for everything. And shipped three apps in thirty days.
This is what I used — and more importantly, why.
First, what does "$0 stack" actually mean?
Let me be honest before I lose you.
Zero dollars doesn’t mean literally free forever. It means zero dollars to start, validate, and launch a working product to real users.
No monthly bills before you have a single user. No server costs until you actually need them. No paying for tools you might not even finish building with.
That was my rule. If it costs money before I have users, I don’t use it.
The stack
React Native + Expo for mobile apps.
Next.js for web and SaaS.
Supabase for database, auth, and storage.
Vercel for deployment.
Claude API for any AI features.
That’s it. No Docker. No separate backend server. No DevOps setup at two in the morning. No AWS bill that gives you a small heart attack.
Why this specific combination
Here’s the thing about stacks — everyone has an opinion. Rails vs Django. Firebase vs Supabase. REST vs GraphQL.
I stopped caring about the debate and started caring about one thing only: how fast can I go from idea to something a real person can open on their phone or browser?
With this stack, the answer is: very fast.
Supabase gives you a Postgres database, authentication, file storage, and even edge functions — all in one place. The free tier is genuinely generous. I’ve launched multiple projects without ever hitting the limit during the validation phase.
Expo with React Native means I write one codebase and it runs on Android and iOS. I’m not a big team. I cannot maintain two separate codebases. Expo solves that problem completely.
Next.js on Vercel is almost unfairly easy to deploy. Push to GitHub, Vercel picks it up, your app is live. No configuration. No server management. It just works.
Claude API for AI features is straightforward to integrate and the output quality is genuinely good for user-facing products.
The three apps and what I learned from each
App one was a daily challenge app. Users get an AI-generated confidence challenge every morning, complete it, and track their streak.
What I learned: Auth and streaks are the core loop. Everything else is decoration. I spent too much time on the UI in the first week. The users who stayed didn’t care about the design — they cared about whether the challenge actually felt personal.
App two was a habit tracker with a twist — it generates a funny "FIR" (police report) every time you skip a habit. Targeted at Pakistani and Indian users who would get the joke immediately.
What I learned: Humor is a retention mechanic. Users were sharing their FIRs on WhatsApp. That’s free distribution. I never bought a single ad. Cultural specificity beats generic design every time.
App three was a client website — a professional service business that needed a fast, clean web presence.
What I learned: Next.js with Tailwind can make something look genuinely premium in a very short time. Clients don’t know what framework you used. They only see the result.
The mistakes that cost me real time
I want to be honest here because most "I built X in Y days" articles skip this part.
Mistake one: I over-engineered the first app.
I added features no user asked for. Notifications, detailed analytics, social sharing — all before anyone had even signed up. I wasted almost a week building things that didn’t matter yet.
The fix: build the smallest version that completes the core promise of the app. Nothing else.
Mistake two: I underestimated AI-generated code.
AI writes code fast. But AI also makes quiet mistakes — wrong folder structures, imported libraries that aren’t installed, duplicate logic hiding in two different files. You don’t catch these immediately. You catch them at two in the morning when something randomly breaks.
The fix: build step by step. Review every piece before moving to the next. Slow down during building so you don’t panic during debugging.
Mistake three: I ignored distribution until the end.
Three working apps. Real features. Decent design. And my initial download numbers were embarrassing.
Shipping is not the finish line. Shipping is just the beginning of a different, harder problem.
What actually matters more than the stack
This is the part I didn’t understand when I started.
The stack is not the moat. The stack is just the vehicle.
What actually determines whether an app succeeds:
Does it solve a real problem someone has today — not a hypothetical problem they might have someday?
Is the core loop so simple that a user understands it within thirty seconds of opening it?
Does it give users a reason to come back tomorrow?
Can you reach the people who need it?
I got the first three right on two of the three apps. I got the fourth one wrong on all three. Distribution is the unsolved problem for me right now — and for most solo developers, if they’re being honest.
The actual cost breakdown after 30 days
Supabase free tier: $0
Vercel free tier: $0
Expo development: $0
Claude API usage during testing: roughly $2–3
Domain names: $10–15 each
Total to launch three working products: under $50.
That number still surprises me when I write it out.
Should you use this exact stack?
Honestly, only if it fits how you think.
If you’re already comfortable with Python, maybe Django and Supabase makes more sense for you. If you’ve been doing Firebase for years, switching costs real time.
The best stack is the one you can move fast with. Not the one the internet tells you is objectively correct.
But if you’re starting fresh and you want something proven, battle-tested, and genuinely beginner-friendly — React Native, Expo, Supabase, Next.js, and Vercel will not let you down.
It hasn’t let me down yet.
One last thing
Thirty days sounds like a tight deadline. It is.
But the constraint is the point.
When you give yourself unlimited time, you add unlimited features. When you give yourself thirty days, you’re forced to ask: what is the one thing this app absolutely must do? And then you build that thing — just that thing — and you ship it.
The stack won’t save you from yourself. But a tight deadline might.
Pick your tools. Set your deadline. Build the smallest version of your idea that actually works.
Then ship it.
The rest you figure out after.

5 min read
Just now
